Summary: Implemented a strict PH Core-aware FHIR inbound parsing layer for the backend webhook flow. The service now validates inbound receive-results and receive-push payloads through per-resource parser modules before mapping and persisting data, and the shared mapper was improved to handle array-shaped encounter code fields from PH Core examples. Added fixture-driven parser and service tests, and verified the backend e2e suite still passes.

Facts:
1. [finished] Added PH Core-aware parser modules under backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers for patient, immunization, encounter, observation, condition, procedure, and medication request payloads.
2. [finished] Parser validation now requires a matching PH Core profile suffix for supported resource types before payloads are accepted.
3. [finished] The FhirSyncService now routes receive-results and receive-push payloads through parseInboundResource before Supabase persistence.
4. [finished] The shared FHIR mapper was updated so encounter codeable-concept text can be read from array-shaped PH Core fields.
5. [finished] New parser/service specs use PH Core fixture JSON files from resources/examples/ph-core and the test suite passes.
6. [finished] Backend e2e tests still pass after the parser/service refactor.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.service.ts — Wired inbound webhook handling through the new parser registry before persistence, keeping route handling thin and validating PH Core payloads first. | Notes: receiveResults and receivePush now parse resources before repository writes.
2. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts — Improved shared mapping logic for array-shaped codeable-concept fields so PH Core encounter payloads normalize correctly. | Notes: Encounter titles now resolve from array-shaped type fields.
3. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.profile.ts — Centralized strict PH Core profile suffix rules for supported FHIR resource families. | Notes: Used by parser modules to validate declared profiles.
4. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/fhir-parser.types.ts — Defined the discriminated union types for parsed inbound patient and clinical resources. | Notes: Keeps parser outputs explicit and typed.
5. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/fhir-parser.utils.ts — Added shared parser boundary helpers for resource-type validation and PH Core profile detection. | Notes: Shared by all resource-specific parsers.
6. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/clinical.parser.ts — Implemented the generic clinical resource parser that validates and normalizes supported non-patient FHIR payloads. | Notes: Delegates resource-specific mapping to the existing mapper.
7. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/patient.parser.ts — Implemented the PH Core patient parser boundary. | Notes: Returns the validated Patient resource for profile updates and identifier matching.
8. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/immunization.parser.ts — Implemented the PH Core immunization parser boundary. | Notes: Wraps the generic clinical parser.
9. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/encounter.parser.ts — Implemented the PH Core encounter parser boundary. | Notes: Wraps the generic clinical parser.
10. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/observation.parser.ts — Implemented the PH Core observation parser boundary. | Notes: Wraps the generic clinical parser.
11. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/condition.parser.ts — Implemented the PH Core condition parser boundary. | Notes: Wraps the generic clinical parser.
12. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/procedure.parser.ts — Implemented the PH Core procedure parser boundary. | Notes: Wraps the generic clinical parser.
13. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/medication-request.parser.ts — Implemented the PH Core medication request parser boundary. | Notes: Wraps the generic clinical parser.
14. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/fhir-parser.registry.ts — Added the parser registry that dispatches inbound resources to the correct resource-specific parser. | Notes: Used by FhirSyncService for receive-results and receive-push.
15. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/fhir-parser.registry.spec.ts — Added fixture-driven parser tests using the PH Core example JSON files. | Notes: Covers patient and clinical resource families plus strict profile rejection.
16. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.service.spec.ts — Added service-level tests proving parsed resources are persisted through the expected repository methods. | Notes: Covers receive-results and receive-push flows.

Summary: Fixed the PowerShell FHIR seed script so it no longer fails on duplicate profile primary keys. The seed flow now treats the profile created by the auth trigger as an upsert target and also upserts patient_identifiers, making reruns idempotent after partial attempts. The script still seeds PH Core example bundles into /fhir/receive-results for immunization, consultation history, medical history, and laboratory results.

Facts:
1. [finished] sqls/001_auth_setup.sql defines an auth.users trigger that automatically syncs a profiles row when a new auth user is created.
2. [finished] scripts/seed-fhir-sync.ps1 now upserts the profile row instead of inserting it, which avoids duplicate profiles_pkey errors after auth-trigger creation or reruns.
3. [finished] scripts/seed-fhir-sync.ps1 also upserts patient_identifiers so the seed script can be rerun without duplicate identifier conflicts.

Affected files:
1. [modified] scripts/seed-fhir-sync.ps1 — Changed the seed flow to upsert profile and patient identifier rows instead of inserting them directly. | Notes: This is the direct fix for the duplicate key error.
2. [inspected] sqls/001_auth_setup.sql — Confirmed the auth.users trigger that auto-creates or updates profiles, explaining the duplicate key failure. | Notes: No code changes were required.
3. [inspected] backend/src/auth/profile.repository.ts — Verified the application uses profile upsert semantics already, which informed the seed script fix. | Notes: No code changes were required.

Summary: Implemented the real outbound interoperability sync submission flow. The backend now sends sync requests to the gateway's /api/v1/fhir/request/{resourceType} endpoint using the configured requester provider ID, selected target provider, and patient identifier, then returns the submitted transaction details. The sync prepare endpoint now responds with HTTP 202 and the tests (unit, build, and e2e) all pass.

Facts:
1. [finished] backend/src/integration/integration.service.ts now performs a real POST to the gateway request endpoint for sync preparation, after validating the selected provider and identifier inputs.
2. [finished] The sync prepare flow now submits requesterId, targetId, patientIdentifiers, reason, and notes to the gateway and parses the returned pending transaction details.
3. [finished] backend/src/integration/integration.controller.ts now returns HTTP 202 Accepted for sync prepare submissions.
4. [finished] backend test/app.e2e-spec.ts and integration service specs were updated to expect the real gateway submission and transaction response.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.service.ts — Implemented the outbound gateway request submission flow, response parsing, and reusable request helpers for sync preparation. | Notes: This is the core behavior change.
2. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.types.ts — Added request submission and transaction response types so the sync flow can return a typed gateway transaction. | Notes: Keeps the contract explicit and stable.
3. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.controller.ts — Changed sync prepare to return HTTP 202 Accepted, matching the semantics of a request submission. | Notes: Aligns the endpoint status with asynchronous processing.
4. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.service.spec.ts — Updated unit tests to verify the gateway request POST and the returned transaction details. | Notes: Covers the new outbound request behavior.
5. [modified] backend/test/app.e2e-spec.ts — Updated e2e coverage for the 202 response and transaction payload on sync prepare. | Notes: Confirms the app-level contract matches the new flow.

content:
Summary: Implemented the first-pass inbound WAH4PC/FHIR sync flow for wah4patients. The backend now has a dedicated fhir-sync module for provider-facing webhooks and practitioner discovery, routes are excluded from the global /api/v1 prefix where needed, patient identifier matching is backed by a new Supabase table, and inbound resources are normalized into the existing internal clinical tables instead of storing raw payloads. Validation and tests passed after the changes.

Facts:
1. [finished] The backend now includes a new fhir-sync module with provider webhook controllers, a gateway auth guard, a sync service, a sync repository, and mapper/types files under backend/src/fhir-sync.
2. [finished] A new Supabase bootstrap file sqls/010_patient_identifiers_setup.sql was added to store patient identifier mappings for matching inbound FHIR payloads to internal profiles.
3. [finished] The backend already had internal tables for immunization_records, medical_history_records, medical_consultation_records, and medication_resupply_history_records, and the new flow writes normalized records into those tables instead of persisting raw FHIR JSON.
4. [finished] Gateway-facing webhook routes are exposed as /api/fhir/practitioners, /fhir/process-query, /fhir/receive-results, and /fhir/receive-push, with the global api/v1 prefix excluded for those paths.
5. [finished] Environment validation now requires WAH4PC_GATEWAY_AUTH_KEY and includes WAH4PC_PRACTITIONER_LIST_ENDPOINT for the provider-side inbound flow.

Summary: Implemented clickable dashboard weekly metric cards and improved BMI startup behavior so the screen no longer flashes the add form before determining whether today's BMI already exists. DashboardMetricCard is now tappable via an optional callback, and DashboardScreen maps BMI, blood pressure, and temperature cards to their existing AppRoutes. BodyMassIndexScreen now sorts loaded history newest-first and renders a loading indicator while history loads, so the add tab immediately becomes the today's-summary state when a record for today is present.

Facts:
1. [finished] DashboardMetricCard now accepts an optional onTap callback and is wrapped in Material + InkWell for card-level navigation feedback.
2. [finished] DashboardScreen now passes tap handlers for BMI, blood pressure, and temperature metric cards that navigate to AppRoutes.bodyMassIndex, AppRoutes.bloodPressure, and AppRoutes.temperature.
3. [finished] BodyMassIndexScreen now sorts fetched BMI history newest-first before storing it and shows a loading indicator while history loads, preventing the add form from flashing before the logged-today state is known.
4. [finished] The BMI screen still uses its existing today-logged summary card flow; no route names were changed and the existing route constants remain the source of navigation.
5. [finished] flutter analyze on the frontend package completed with only pre-existing warnings/info unrelated to these changes.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/dashboard/presentation/widgets/dashboard_metric_card.dart — Added tap support so the weekly metric cards can behave as navigable targets while keeping the widget reusable and presentation-focused. | Notes: Wrapped the existing card body in Material + InkWell and exposed onTap.
2. [modified] frontend/lib/features/dashboard/presentation/screens/dashboard_screen.dart — Wired the weekly health metric cards to route to the correct personal-record screens without changing route constants or moving navigation into shared components. | Notes: Added a small route-mapping helper and passed onTap callbacks into DashboardMetricCard.
3. [modified] frontend/lib/features/phr/body_mass_index/presentation/screen/body_mass_index_screen.dart — Prevented the BMI screen from showing the add form before history is loaded and made the latest-entry detection resilient to API ordering. | Notes: Added loading UI for the add tab and sorted fetched history newest-first before setting state.

Summary: Implemented stricter field-type validation for the Personal Information flow. The frontend profile form now rejects digits in text fields and letters in numeric fields through validators, and the backend DTOs enforce the same rules for names, communication language, city/province/country, optional personal detail text fields, phone numbers, postal code, PhilHealth/PhilSys IDs, and emergency contact phone. Address lines remain mixed alphanumeric for practical street-address support. Focused backend DTO tests and the Flutter Personal Information screen test passed, and the Kanban card was moved to done.

Summary: Implemented Phase 2 of the interoperability rollout across backend and Flutter. The backend now exposes a WAH4PC-backed interoperability proxy for provider discovery and sync-request preparation, with env validation and e2e coverage. The Flutter app now launches a dedicated sync wizard from the Personal Information screen, uses bottom-sheet selection for identifier/provider choice, and reuses the existing profile readiness rules to gate entry. Tests for the new interoperability models, wizard flow, and profile-to-wizard navigation all pass.

Facts:
1. [finished] The backend interoperability proxy now exposes GET /api/v1/interoperability/providers and POST /api/v1/interoperability/sync/prepare, backed by the real WAH4PC gateway endpoint wah4pc.echosphere.cfd.
2. [finished] The backend environment contract now requires WAH4PC_GATEWAY_URL, WAH4PC_API_KEY, and WAH4PC_PROVIDER_ID, and those values are documented in backend/.env.example and backend/README.md.
3. [finished] The Flutter sync flow now launches a dedicated SyncRecordsWizardScreen from the Personal Information sync button, instead of showing only a placeholder snackbar.
4. [finished] The Flutter wizard uses existing bottom-sheet selection controls to pick a PhilHealth or PhilSys identifier and then an active provider, while preserving profile readiness gating from Phase 1.
5. [finished] A Flutter test harness file was added to mock flutter_secure_storage calls globally so AuthSession.clear() can run in widget tests without a plugin exception.

Summary: Reviewed the interoperability rollout docs and backlog to shape Phase 2. The intended implementation boundary is a new sync wizard started from Personal Information, with identifier selection and provider selection handled as separate steps. Provider discovery and readiness validation should live in a dedicated interoperability module rather than inside the profile screen. The backlog item for Phase 2 was updated to reflect this narrowed scope.

Facts:
1. [finished] Phase 2 should start from the Personal Information sync action and open a dedicated multi-step sync wizard rather than adding provider selection directly into the profile editor.
2. [finished] Identifier selection for the sync flow should remain compatible with PhilHealth ID or PhilSys ID, and the selection UX can use a bottom sheet inside the wizard.
3. [finished] Provider discovery is documented as a gateway/backend concern, so frontend code should consume a backend provider endpoint instead of hardcoding provider options.

Summary: Implemented read-only Supabase-backed Health Records storage integration. Added SQL schema for a single health_records table designed for manual Supabase inserts and future backend create endpoints, added backend database typings and a read-only NestJS HealthRecords module, and refactored Flutter Health Records screens to load authenticated records from the backend without adding any create/receive endpoints. Validation passed with backend npm run build and frontend flutter analyze.

Facts:
1. [finished] Health Records now use a new Supabase table named public.health_records with section_key, title/subtitle, summary fields, filter/status fields, status/accent tone keys, icon_key, details_json, display_order, recorded_at, created_at, and updated_at.
2. [finished] The backend exposes a read-only GET /health-records?section=<section_key> endpoint protected by the existing API key guard and bearer access-token user resolution; no POST/create endpoint was added.
3. [finished] The Flutter Health Records screens no longer contain hardcoded HealthRecordEntry sample data; they use a shared HealthRecordSectionScreen that loads records by section and maps API responses into the existing HealthRecordScreenTemplate.
4. [finished] Manual Supabase inserts should use section_key values medical_history, immunization_records, medical_consultations, or laboratory_results and details_json as an array of objects with label and value string fields.

content:
Summary: Reviewed the frontend Health Records screens and the existing Personal Records architecture to plan a future local persistence layer for Health Records. The current Health Records feature is still presentation-driven with hardcoded entry data in the screen files, while Personal Records already has a dedicated typed data/repository pattern. The recommended approach is to introduce a separate local health-records repository and domain model layer so the UI can read from a single source of truth now and later support creation without reworking the screens.

Facts:
1. [finished] The frontend Health Records feature currently stores its record entries directly inside the screen files, especially the Medical History, Immunization Records, Medical Consultations, and Laboratory Results screens.
2. [finished] The Health Record screen template already supports filtering, search, and expansion, so it can stay as the presentation layer while data sourcing moves behind a repository.

content:
Summary: Added a plain-English atlas for WAH4P so future work can read the project like a map. The repo is best understood in layers: a Flutter client that controls navigation and presentation, a NestJS backend that owns security and business rules, and Supabase/Postgres plus secure local storage for persistence. The most important user journeys now have an explicit flow: splash/startup -> onboarding or login -> dashboard or MPIN unlock -> feature areas like records, appointments, resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. This memory also clarifies which screens are static, which are route-driven, and which backend APIs support each major journey.

Facts:
1. [finished] Think of the repository as three layers: frontend/ is the Flutter presentation and navigation layer, backend/ is the NestJS security and business-logic layer, and sqls/ plus the data-structure docs define the Supabase/Postgres persistence layer.
2. [finished] The app begins at the Flutter shell in frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart, which owns theme setup, route generation, startup gating, and resume-time MPIN locking; the visible launch flow is controlled from there rather than from individual screens.
3. [finished] Startup behavior is intentionally simple to follow: the app checks whether a valid session exists, whether onboarding is complete, and whether MPIN unlock is required, then routes the user to onboarding, login, dashboard, or the MPIN unlock screen.
4. [finished] The Flutter route system is centralized in frontend/lib/app/app_routes.dart and frontend/lib/app/app_router.dart, so route names and screen mappings should be edited there first when changing navigation.
5. [finished] The route map forms a clear product atlas: splash and onboarding lead into registration and login, then into the dashboard, and from there into health records, personal records, appointments, medication resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, security settings, and legal/privacy screens.
6. [finished] The frontend feature folders mirror product domains, with separate areas for auth, onboarding, dashboard, health_records, phr, appointments, calendar, medication_resupply, profile, notification, legal, and splash.
7. [finished] The auth feature is one of the largest frontend domains and includes the registration screens, login, email verification, forgot password, MPIN setup/unlock, TOTP challenge/setup, and security verification flows.
8. [finished] The backend app module in backend/src/app.module.ts wires configuration, throttling, the health module, and the auth module, while global guards enforce API-key access and rate limiting across the server.
9. [finished] The backend health endpoint is intentionally public and small, while the rest of the server is protected unless a route is marked public with the custom decorator.
10. [finished] The backend auth module is the server-side workflow hub and splits responsibilities into registration, login, password reset, profile, TOTP, MPIN, and support services/repositories instead of one monolithic auth implementation.
11. [finished] The backend auth controller groups the API by user journey: registration OTP request/verify/complete, password reset request/verify/complete, login and refresh, TOTP setup and verification, security verification, MPIN setup/verification/device registration, and device unregister flow.
12. [finished] The backend profile controller is separate from the login flow and handles authenticated profile retrieval and updates via /profile/me, which matches the frontend profile and personal information screens.
13. [finished] The project’s data rules are to keep canonical patient data in Supabase/Postgres, keep session/onboarding/device flags in secure local storage, and treat dashboard summaries and similar UI aggregates as derived data rather than separate source records.
14. [finished] Some documentation files are secondary references rather than the live truth, especially the older screen inventory; for navigation work, the live Flutter route constants and route builder are the authoritative map.

Summary: Saved a deeper project map for WAH4P that focuses on navigation, feature boundaries, and service responsibilities. The repository presents a clear two-tier architecture: a NestJS backend that owns auth/security/business rules and a Flutter frontend that owns display, navigation, and device-scoped state. content:
Summary: Audited the WAH4P repository and saved a clear project snapshot for future work. The repo is split into a NestJS backend, a Flutter frontend, and supporting SQL/docs. The backend is a secured API with global API-key protection, throttling, strict env validation, Supabase auth/data integration, and Resend email delivery. The frontend is a Flutter mobile app with centralized theming, secure local storage, a startup gate, and MPIN/session lifecycle handling. The docs describe a Supabase/Postgres source of truth for patient data, with frontend screens mostly acting as presentation and interaction layers. I also captured the current testing surface and a couple of documentation mismatches that future work should reconcile.

Facts:
1. [finished] The repository is organized as a multi-part WAH4P project with backend/, frontend/, sqls/, and documentation files at the root, so future changes should treat it as a full-stack product rather than a single app.
2. [finished] The backend is a NestJS API under backend/ that uses Supabase for auth/data, Resend for email OTP delivery, global API-key protection, throttling, and strict Joi-based environment validation.
3. [finished] The backend HTTP entrypoint sets the global API prefix to /api/v1, enables helmet, applies a whitelist/transform ValidationPipe, and reads CORS origins from FRONTEND_ORIGIN.
4. [finished] The frontend is a Flutter app under frontend/ with a centralized Material theme, Open Sans typography, secure storage, and a startup gate flow driven by app state and route generation.
5. [finished] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart manages app lifecycle behavior, including MPIN lock handling on resume by checking local device state and backend security status before showing the unlock route.
6. [finished] Project docs in data-structure.md and checklist.md define Supabase/Postgres as the canonical source of truth for patient data, while local storage is reserved for session, onboarding, and device-scoped flags.
7. [finished] The repo has active test coverage in both layers: backend has Jest unit/e2e scaffolding, and frontend has multiple widget/screen tests under frontend/test.
8. [finished] SCREENS.md and some docs appear to reflect an older source tree or source-of-truth path, so documentation should be reconciled with the current repository layout before relying on it as the only inventory.
